coredata.dc:title: Ternary nanocomposite of GQDs-polyFc/Fe3O4/PANI: Design, synthesis, and applied for electrochemical energy storage
coredata.dc:creator: Payami, Elmira
Teimuri-Mofrad, Reza
coredata.dc:description: Redox-active and conductive polymers and transition metal oxides are appearing group of faradaic battery-type electrode materials that exhibit improved electrochemical properties in supercapacitors. Herein, we report a new class of ferrocenyl-based nanocomposite electrodes for the development of high-performance supercapacitors using a simple physical mixing method of redox active ferrocenyl modified GQDs with conductive polymer including PANI and Fe3O4. Having the popular redox-active organometallic compound like (Fc), PANI, and Fe3O4 as integrated constituent components, the obtained electrode materials exhibit remarkably charge storage and delivery capabilities. The structural properties of the electrode materials investigate by appropriate analyses. The highest capacity, energy density, and power density are to be 295 mAh g−1, 62.1 Wh kg−1 and 4140 W kg−1 for the nanocomposite, respectively. These results which can be attributed to the synergistic behavior of the different types of electrode materials in the hybrid nanocomposites, leading to the improved electrochemical performance. In addition, the cycling performance is tested with 5000 long cycles. The retention of charge storage (91.45%) after 5000 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1 shows the great potential of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposites as electrode materials for future energy storage technologies. Finally, we conclude with some suggestions for future work.

head.ce:title: Ternary nanocomposite of GQDs-polyFc/Fe3O4/PANI: Design, synthesis, and applied for electrochemical energy storage

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Redox-active and conductive polymers and transition metal oxides are appearing group of faradaic battery-type electrode materials that exhibit improved electrochemical properties in supercapacitors. Herein, we report a new class of ferrocenyl-based nanocomposite electrodes for the development of high-performance supercapacitors using a simple physical mixing method of redox active ferrocenyl modified GQDs with conductive polymer including PANI and Fe3O4. Having the popular redox-active organometallic compound like (Fc), PANI, and Fe3O4 as integrated constituent components, the obtained electrode materials exhibit remarkably charge storage and delivery capabilities. The structural properties of the electrode materials investigate by appropriate analyses. The highest capacity, energy density, and power density are to be 295 mAh g−1, 62.1 Wh kg−1 and 4140 W kg−1 for the nanocomposite, respectively. These results which can be attributed to the synergistic behavior of the different types of electrode materials in the hybrid nanocomposites, leading to the improved electrochemical performance. In addition, the cycling performance is tested with 5000 long cycles. The retention of charge storage (91.45%) after 5000 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1 shows the great potential of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposites as electrode materials for future energy storage technologies. Finally, we conclude with some suggestions for future work.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, in view of the rapid expansion of the world economy, increasing environmental pollution and dwindling energy resources, many scientists have shown great interest in the development of clean, cheap and renewable energy, such as supercapacitors [1–3]. Among various energy storage devices, supercapacitors, also known as ultracapacitors, have promising application potential due to their high power density, excellent lifetime, good reversibility and safe operation, although they have some drawbacks such as low energy density [4,5]. Nevertheless, great efforts have been made to overcome the main obstacle of low energy density without compromising cycle performance or power density [6–11].

In general, supercapacitors based on charge storage mechanisms can be divided into electrochemical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors (PCs). The first class of capacitors has excellent power capability but low energy density, which mostly use carbon-based nanomaterials with a large surface area to store energy at the electrode/electrolyte surface. Generally, graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and so on exhibit this kind of energy storage mechanism. The prepare of hybrid nanoscale supercapacitorhas opened up novel rang of vision towards a deeper study of EDLCs processes and constructed more impressive nano-storage devices. Notably, the development of redox-active battery-type nanomaterials bearing pseudocapacitance, including ferrocene (Fc), metal oxides, and conductive polymers has recently been investigated. These redox-active materials exhibit a fast and reversible faradaic redox reaction that occurs at the surface of the electrode materials and found that they can be classified into the later groups of supercapacitors [12–18]. To achieve high energy and power densities, EDLCs and PCs are used together in various applications such as hybrid devices. It is very difficult to achieve both high power density and high energy density in a single device. But the efforts of scientists have been significantly expanded to develop similar storage devices using appropriate redox-active battery-type materials and design methods. Clearly, supercapacitors with battery-type properties could meet this need to some extent. Therefore, a numerous of pathways have been attempted to design energy storage devices with increased electrochemical performances constructed from various metal oxides including iron, manganese, cobalt or etc. nanostructures. The prepare of binder-free electrode materials, surface-modified metal oxides by EDLCs materials or conductive polymers by utilizing various pathways like core-shell, core-branch, and sandwich structure as well as the anchorage of biomolecules onto the various metal oxides are among the most lately related reported research works [19–22].

According to various research reports, transition metal oxides such as MnO2 [23], NiO [24], Fe3O4 [25], RuO2 [26], and V2O5 [27] exhibit good supercapacitor performances. Among these metal oxides, Fe3O4 nanoparticles have shown high conductivity and remarkable electrochemical performances. Fe3O4 is a remarkable electrode nanomaterial due to its outstanding properties such as natural abundance, environmental friendliness, and low cost [28,29]. Wang et al. [30] prepared Fe3O4 with large surface area by a hydrothermal approach and investigated their electrochemical activities. The supercapacitor performance of MNPs-based electrode materials such as Fe3O4/Fe-CNT [31], G/ Fe3O4@Fe/ZnO [32], Fe3O4@FLG [33], and Fe3O4-C hybrid nanocomposites [34] has been investigated by various research groups.

Conductive polymers such as polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole (PPy), polythiophene (PT), and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) are characterized by excellent flexibility, high conductivity, and simple and easy synthetic methods. Despite these suitable properties, these conductive polymers suffer from their low stability. Among the above polymers, PANI exhibits high charge storage capacity. It also exhibits various oxidation forms, including a fully reduced form as well as a fully oxidized and a semi-oxidized form. The morphology of this polymer can be controlled by various synthesis methods. Due to the synergistic effects between PANI and various metal oxides, metal oxide polymer nanocomposites show improved supercapacitor performance [35–38]. PANI based electrode materials such as RuO2/PANI [39], NiO/PANI [40], Fe2O3/PANI [41], NiCo2O4@PANI [42], MnOx/PANI [43], MoS2/PANI [44] have shown excellent electrochemical properties. The incorporation PANI as a popular conductive polymer onto the other electrode materials has given rise multifunctional electrode materials that facilitate rapid ion penetration and charge-transfer process at the surface of electrode/electrolyte, also an enhance in the number of active sites via the N atoms [45].

In recent decades, organometallic materials have attracted much attention and are at the forefront of chemistry, as evidenced by the discovery of ferrocene (Fc) with outstanding redox properties [46]. The stable and good interaction between Fe2+ and cyclopentadienyl rings provides excellent thermal stability and helps us to synthesize various Fc-based compounds [47]. Fc quickly attracted the attention of scientists due to its special chemical properties. They began to improve synthetic approaches based on Fc-containing compounds and explored its use in a variety of scientific fields. Fc is a redox-active organometallic compound that exhibits both metallic and nonmetallic properties. Due to its unique electronic properties, Fc and its derivatives such as Fc-containing redox-active polymers have attracted great interest in electrochemical fields such as supercapacitors, sensors, catalysts and so on [48–63].

In general, excellent energy storage electrode materials show magnificent electrochemical properties. Based on previous reports [64], we have found that GQDs exhibit excellent stability and electrochemical performance. Accordingly, a new nanocomposite for energy storage was prepared by modifying GQDs with redox active electrode materials.

In the present work, to improve the electrochemical performance of GQDs, we modified their surface with Fc-containing polymers to obtain a redox-active nanohybrid. Then, a ternary GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposite was prepared by a simple physical mixing method. All synthesized nanomaterials were evaluated by appropriate techniques including the proton nuclear magnetic resonance technique (1H NMR), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman spectroscopy (532 nm), X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and an energy dispersive X-ray beam (EDX). After trustworthy characterization of the resulted electrode materials, the electrochemical performances of the as-synthesized nanomaterials were firstly investigated in a three electrode configuration using appropriate techniques such as CV, GCD, and EIS experiments. Finally, the electrochemical performance of the final nanocomposite was elucidated in a symmetric supercapacitor device consisting of as-synthesized GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposite as both positive and negative electrodes.

All materials and solvents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich or Merck and were reagent grade and used without any purification. GQDs were synthesized by an effective pyrolysis approach to carbonize citric acid (CA) [64]. Also, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and PANI were papered according to the reported approaches [50].

To a stirred suspension of AlCl3 (2.2 g, 16.5 mmol) in DCM (15 ml) was added dropwise a solution of 3-Chloropropinoyl chloride (2.1 g, 16.5 mmol) in DCM (15 ml) and the reaction mixture was stirred at 5 °C for one hour. The obtained AlCl3/3-Chloropropinoyl chloride complex was slowly added to a solution of Fc (1.42 g, 16.5 mmol) in DCM (15 ml) at 0 °C and then allowed to warm to ambient temperature and stirred for another 16 hours. After this time, 15 ml of solution containing 0.68 g (18.15 mmol) NaBH4 and 7 ml diglyme were slowly added to the above mixture. After the mentioned addition, an orange color was produced followed by stirring for 2 h. After water hydrolysis, the organic phase was separated and the aqueous phase was extracted with DCM. The combined organic phases were then washed with brine. After evaporation of DCM, the diglyme and unreacted Fc were removed from the reaction medium by vacuum distillation. Finally, 3-chloropropylferrocene was obtained after purification by column chromatography (n-hexane) [65,66].

An amount of 0.1 g (1.14 mmol) NaOAc and 0.6 g (0.38 mmol) amount of NaI were added to a stirred solution of 3-Chloropropylferrocene (0.1 g, 0.38 mmol) in DMF (4 ml) and then heated to 120 °C. After completion of the reaction (monitored by TLC), the resulting mixture was poured into water and extracted with DCM. After evaporation of the solvent, a solution of K2CO3 in methanol was added to the crude product and stirred at room temperature for 2 h. After full completion of the hydrolysis reaction, the medium was extracted with DCM, dried over anhydrous Na2SO4, and evaporated on a rotary apparatus and purified by column chromatography. 3-Ferrocenylpropanol was obtained as a dark brown viscous oil (94%) [57].

A round bottom two-necked flask was filled with 7.8 g (32 mmol) 3-ferrocenylpropanol, 0.6 g (0.16 mmol) TBAI, and 1.92 g (48 mmol) NaOH and then epichlorohydrin (4.4 g, 48 mmol) was added via a dropping funnel and stirred at 25 °C for 7 h. Then water (80 ml) was added to the reaction medium and extracted with DCM, dried over anhydrous Na2SO4 and evaporated on a rotary apparatus and purified by column chromatography. 2-[(3-Ferrocenylpropoxy)methyl]oxirane was obtained as a dark brown viscous oil (89%) [67].

The cationic ring-opening polymerization method was used to synthesis this polymer [67]. A dry Schlenk tube was charged with 0.02 g (0.22 mmol) of 1,4-butanediol and 10 ml of dry toluene. The resulting solution was degassed by three freeze-pump-thaw cycles and then 0.005 g (0.03 mmol) of BF3.Et2O was slowly added to the reaction flask under N2 protection, followed by stirring at 25 °C for 1 hour. The reaction flask was then transferred to an ice bath and a mixture of 0.03 g (0.33 mmol) of epichlorohydrin and 1 g (3.33 mmol) of 2-[(3-ferrocenylpropoxy)methyl]oxirane were slowly added to the reaction medium. After 8 hours, a small amount of water was added and the resulting solution was washed with sodium bicarbonate solution (5% wt/v) and distilled water until the pH reached 7. The solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure and the obtained polymer was dried under vacuum at 50 °C.

A solution of synthezsized PolyFc in DMF was added to a suspension of GQDs in DMF and then stirred for 72 h. After this period, the GQDs-PolyFc was obtained after centrifugation and sequential washing with DMF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc/PANI, and GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc/PANI, and GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposites in the ratio of 1:1 GQDs-PolyFc:Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc:PANI, and GQDs-PolyFc:Fe3O4:PANI, a suspension of each component in acetone was added to the suspension of the GQDs-PolyFc nanohybrid and then sonicated for 4 hours. The final samples were obtained after evaporation of the solvent.

To obtain a complete understanding of the chemical composition of the synthesized compounds, the proton nuclear magnetic resonance technique (1H NMR, 400 MHz) was employed using CDCl3 as solvent. The chemical structure of the as-prepared electrode materials was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR (Bruker tensor 270)). Raman spectroscopy (532 nm) was used to study the vibrational properties of as-synthesized nanomaterials. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the samples was performed using a PANalytica X pertPRO (Germany). The surface morphology and elemental composition of the compounds were studied using field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM (MIRA3 TESCAN)) and an energy dispersive X-ray beam (EDX (MIRA3 TESCAN)).

Electrochemical experiments such as CV, GCD, and EIS were performed at an electrochemical workstation using 1M H2SO4 as the supporting electrolyte. The working electrodes were prepared by dispersing 80% of the active materials, 10% acetylene black and 10% polyvinylidene fluoride in NMP. In a three-electrode configuration, the as-synthesized electrode materials, the Ag/AgCl electrode and a Pt wire electrode were used as the working electrode, reference electrode and auxiliary electrode, respectively. The value of specific capacity was estimated using Eqs. (1) and (2) based on CV and GCD, respectively:

To investigate the practical applications of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode material, we constructed a novel symmetric supercapacitor with coupling carbon paper as the separator between the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI sample as the anode and cathode electrodes and H2SO4 1M as the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of as-synthesized electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: The polymerization process is exhibited in Scheme 1 . Estimated amounts of 1,4-butanediol as polymerization initiator, BF3.Et2O as catalyst, and dry toluene as solvent were subjected into Schlenk tube. The Schlenk tube was then connected to a vacuum line and degassed with three freeze-pump-thaw cycles followed by stirring for 1 h at 25 °C to form an active condensate catalyst as indicated in Scheme 1. Then, the estimated amounts of monomers mixture were introduced into the reaction vessel and were polymerized involving a cationic ring-opening polymerization for 8h. Finally, after the washing with sodium bicarbonate solution and solvent evaporation, the synthesized polymer was obtained as dark-brown viscous oil.

The as-synthesized polymer was evaluated using 1H NMR and the obtained spectrum is shown in Fig. 1 . The 1H NMR spectrum of the as-prepared polymer exhibited signals of CH2-Cl, CH-O and CH2-O in the back-bone of polymer, which were overlapped over each other and appeared at the range of 3.41-3.97 ppm. The appearing broad peak appeared at 2.5 ppm is due to the OH functional groups in the polymer back-bone. The proton signals appearing at the range of 1.64–1.70 ppm are assigned to the central units of butanediol. The proton signals at 1.75–1.82 ppm were attributed to the O-CH2CH 2 on the alkyl side chain of Fc. A multiple peaks appeared at 2.36–2.40 ppm was assigned to the -CH 2-Cp protons adjacent to the Fc ring and the appearing peaks at the range of 4.04–4.09 ppm are attributed to the Fc ring.

The FT-IR spectrum of the Fc-containing polymer is depicted in Fig. 2 a. The as-prepared polymer exhibits a broad peak at about 3500 cm−1, corresponding to the O-H functional groups. Absorption peaks at about 3100 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, and 490 cm−1 assignable to Ar-H, CC, and Cp-Fe, respectively, indicated the presence of the Fc moiety in the synthesized polymer. Electrochemical performance of the Fc-containing polymer was performed in 0.1 M LiCLO4/CH3CN within the potential range of 0 to 0.8 V versus Ag/AgCl at various scan rates (10–100 mV s−1). As indicated in Fig. 2b, the CV curves show one pair of reversible redox peaks, corresponding to the reversible redox reaction of Fc moiety. To evaluate the reversibility of the as-synthesized polymer, the CV curves at varying sweep rates were measured, as exhibited in Fig. 2b. It is clear that both the oxidation and reduction peak currents are corresponding to the square root of sweep rates in the range of 10–100 mV s−1 and show a linear relationship, implying that the electrochemical reaction at the as-synthesized electrode is a diffusion-controlled process (Fig. 2c).

Comparative FT-IR spectra of GQDs, GQDs-PolyFc, Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4, PANI, GQDs-PolyFc/PANI, GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 4 a. FT-IR spectrum of GQDs exhibits absorption bonds at about 3400 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibration of -OH, and CO functional groups, respectively. The appeared peaks at around 1600 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 indicates the presence of CC and C-O functional groups on the surface of GQDs [68]. After modification of surface of GQDs with Fc-containing polymer, the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-PolyFc shows some new peaks at 1134 cm−1 (CC), 1074 cm−1 and 814 cm−1 (C-H), and 425 cm−1 (Cp-Fe) confirming the successful modifying of GQDs surface [69]. FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4 exhibits absorption bands of both GQDs-PolyFc and Fe3O4 indicating formation of related nanohybrid. The absorption peak at 579 cm−1 is assigned to Fe-O stretching vibration [70]. The principal characteristic bands in pure PANI appeared at 1490 cm−1 and 1577 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of benzenoid and quinoid segment of PANI, respectively. The wide absorption peak was appeared about 3400 cm−1 might be ascribed to the N-H functional group. The absorption bands at 1300 cm−1 and 809 cm−1 attributed to stretching vibration of C-N and out of plane bending of C-H, respectively [71]. The FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs-PolyFc/PANI also exhibits all peaks of GQDs-PolyFc and PANI thus prove formation of GQDs-PolyFc/PANI sample. FT-IR spectrum of the final nanocomposite exhibits characteristic absorption bonds of Fc moiety at 1139 cm−1 (C=C), and 826 cm−1 (C-H), and 491 cm−1 (Cp-Fe). Also, the appeared peaks at 3446 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 can be assigned to stretching vibrations of -OH, and CO functional groups, respectively. In addition, the presence main characteristic peaks of pure PANI at 1490 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, which can be attributed to hydrogen bonded NH bond between amine and imine sites in the FT-IR spectrum of the final nanocomposite as well as the appeared absorption peak at 581 cm−1 that can be assigned to FeO stretching vibration, indicating the successful formation of ternary GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposite.

Fig. 4b exhibits the Raman spectra of the all synthesized electrode materials. Raman experiment is an important test to evaluate the quality of as-prepared GQDs. The D and G bands properties exhibit valuable information about the successful preparation of the GQDs. The mentioned bands are related to the disorder and defect in the hexagonal lattice (D band) and the vibrations of sp2 C atom (G band). The Raman spectrum of GQDs-PolyFc (Fig. 4b) exhibits characteristic D (1377 cm−1) and G (1564 cm−1) bands [72]. Additionally, the Raman spectrum of GQDs-PolyFc reveals characteristic A1g ring metal stretch at 321 cm−1, E1g symmetry ring peak at 398 cm−1, and overlapping peak (E2g CC stretch) at 1561 cm−1 [73]. Raman spectrum of PANI also exhibits various mid-intensity peaks in the low wavenumber region such as 521 cm−1, 743 cm−1, and 777 cm−1, which associated with the amine in-plane deformation and imine deformation, respectively. Also, the Raman spectrum of PANI shows the most intense Raman bands, which splited in various region of wave number including 1500–1600 cm−1 (assigned to the CC ring stretching vibration) and 1300–1400 cm−1 (C-N stretching vibration) [45,74]. The Raman spectrum of Fe3O4 shows well defined peaks at 291 cm−1, 364 cm−1, 505 cm−1, and 668 cm−1 [75]. In addition, all of the main Raman peaks of nanocomposites are shifted to lower values, indicating the strong non-covalent interactions between nanocomposite components [45].

FE-SEM experiment was used to provide the details of the surface structure of the as-prepared and the obtained micrographs are shown in Fig. 5 . The FE-SEM image of GQDs-PolyFc (Fig. 5a) shows the aggregate structure after surface modification of GQDs. The formation of these nano-aggregates can be attributed to the π-π interactions and hydrogen bonding between Fc-containing polymer and GQDs surface [64]. The FE-SEM image of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 5b) reveals that these nanoparticles are homogeneous and have sphere-like architectures. Fig. 5c demonstrates uniform distribution of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles into the GQDs. The porous inherent and the clumped spherical morphology of the pure PANI was indicated from Fig. 5d. From the FE-SEM picture in Fig. 5e., it can be seen that the surface of the GQDs-PolyFc/PANI are rougher than the GQDs-PolyFc, indicating the dispersion of the pure PANI into the nanohybrid. The highly porous structure of the final nanocomposite was confirmed by the FE-SEM image of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposite (Fig. 5f), that provide much active sites for faradaic battery-type redox reaction and facilitate the transport and penetration of ions into the electrode materials, leading to the prominent electrochemical performances of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI sample.

The presence of corresponding elements in the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI nanocomposite and its chemical composition was then evaluated using EDX technique and the obtained data are represented in Fig. 6 . The EDX curve of the final nanocomposite demonstrates the peaks of C, N, O, Cl, and Fe elements at their related KeV value. The element distribution in the corresponding nanocomposite was investigated by applying EDX-mapping as represented in Fig. 6. The resulted image depicts C, N, O, Cl, and Fe elements were uniformly distributed over the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI sample, indicating the homogeneity of the final nanocomposite.

The XRD measurement was used to realize the crystallinity properties of the as-prepared electrode materials. For the present work, XRD patterns of the all samples were compared together to monitor the changes in crystal structures (Fig. 7 ). The XRD pattern of pure GQDs shows one broad and low intense peaks at 2θ∼25° [76], whilst the XRD pattern of GQDs-PolyFc exhibits a change in the crystalline structure of GQDs after modification of its surface with Fc-containing polymer. The main group of related Fc-containing polymer low intense peaks in XRD pattern is generally placed in the region of around 2θ∼10-40°, which overlapped with the broad peak of pure GQDs, indicating amorphous structure of GQDs-PolyFc sample. The Fe3O4-XRD pattern was matching with the JCPDS card number (19-0629), for the crystalline magnetic nanoparticles [77]. The XRD pattern of pure PANI exhibits a broad diffraction peak at 2θ∼22°, which correlate with (200) plane of this polymer [78]. The XRD patterns of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4 and GQDs-PolyFc/PANI nanocomposites show the broad amorphous diffraction peak, which related to the both GQDs-PolyFc and PANI. The final ternary nanocomposite, in addition to showing the mentioned broad diffraction peak, also shows the peaks related to the Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which is a confirmation of the successful synthesis of the final nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performances of the as-prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: Before the construction of the symmetric cell, the electrochemical experiment was performed with a three-electrode system. The obtained data of the ternary nanocomposite GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI were compared with GQDs, GQDs-PolyFc, Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4, PANI, GQDs-PolyFc/PANI samples measured at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in a potential range from −0.2 to 1 V are shown in Fig. 8 a. The CV curve of the GQDs doesn't show much CV current with a nearly rectangular CV shape with no clear redox peaks within the same potential window. This illustrates the EDLC performance of the pure GQDs, where the energy storage mechanism is based on the adsorption/desorption of ions on the electrode surface. Interestingly, CVs derived from all surface-modified GQDs electrodes show distinct differences as well as extraordinary pseudo-capacity performance. The CV curve of GQDs-PolyFc shows a pair of redox peaks centered at about −0.3 V and 0.6 V results from the superposition of the Faradaic redox peaks from PolyFc in H2SO4 solution, demonstrating the excellent contribution of the Faradaic battery-type nature of Fc-containing polymer, leading to an increased specific capacity of this electrode after the surface modification of GQDs with Fc-containing polymer. However, the broad redox peaks in both the anodic and cathodic scans provided a larger loop area, indicating the existence of EDLC. Each nanocomposite shows a slightly various current response depending on its composition. The CV curves of Fe3O4 and GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4 consist of a pair of redox peaks, which related to the reversible redox reactions of Fe(II)↔Fe(III). The CV curve of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4 electrode shows a larger CV area compared to Fe3O4, which obviously indicates the higher specific capacity of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4 electrode material. This finding can be attributed to the improved conductivity obtained by blending with ferrocenyl-modified GQDs. The GQDs-PolyFc/PANI exhibits reversible redox peaks in a potential windows characteristic for PANI-based nanomaterials related to the emeraldine/pernigraniline transition. It is obviously observed that the CV response of both anodic and cathodic redox peaks remarkably enhance in the ternary final nanocomposite. The presence of redox-active materials including Fc as a battery-type redox-active organometallic compound, PANI as a conductive polymer, and Fe3O4 as a battery-type metal oxide are the source of the high capacities measured. It is clear that the incorporation of the Fc moiety as a Faradaic redox-active material into the GQDs surface can increase the specific capacity of the electrode materials. In the case of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode, the increased specific capacity is attributed to the synergistic effect between GQDs-PolyFc, Fe3O4, and PANI sample. The EDLC contribution of the GQDs and the faradaic battery-type contribution of the Fc-containing polymer, Fe3O4, and PANI together lead to an increase in the specific capacity of the final nanocomposite. Here, besides the GQDs, Fe3O4, and PANI also have excellent conductivity and together they increase the conductivity of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode. Another important factor to consider when using composite materials in supercapacitors is their rate capability. To evaluate the capacity behavior and rate capability of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode, the CV curves were measured at different sweep rates (Fig. 8b). The faradaic battery-type nature of the CV curve was maintained even when the sweep rate was increased from 10 to 150 mV s−1, indicating the excellent rate capability of the final nanocomposite. Moreover, the CV response and sweep rate show a linear relationship, indicating rapid charge transfer through the electrode/electrolyte surface. It is perfectly normal that the efficient interaction of the electrolyte ions with the active materials decreases as the sweep rate increases, because they do not have enough time to penetrate thoroughly. As a result, the separation between redox peaks will greatly increase. However, as shown in Fig. 8b, the separation of redox peaks is small and the redox property is maintained even at higher sweep rates, reflecting the insignificant polarization and excellent rate capability of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode. Also, the specific capacity of all electrode materials at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 were calculated using Eq. (1). At a sweep rate of 50 mV s−1, GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode yield a value of 289 mAh g−1. Whilst GQDs, GQDs-PolyFc, Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4, PANI, GQDs-PolyFc/PANI were able to show only 25.3, 117.8, 95.4, 171, 101.6, and 187 mAh g−1, respectively. The redox active properties of Fc-containing polymer, Fe3O4, and PANI and synergistic effect between mentioned component lead to enhance the specific capacity of the final nanocomposite

Fig. 9 a shows the GCD plots for each sample at a current density of 2.5 A g−1. Obviously, the charging section is symmetrical to the corresponding discharge equivalents, indicating the admirable coulombic efficiency. The observed curvature during both charging and discharging is in exact agreement with the CV curves. The discharge time of the samplesincreases in the order of GQDs < GQDs-PolyFc < GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4 < GQDs-PolyFc/PANI < GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI, indicating the extreme improvement in specific capacity by mixing GQDs-PolyFc, Fe3O4 and PANI. The specific capacity values of all electrode materials can be calculated using Eq. (2). The highest specific capacity values for GQDs, GQDs-PolyFc, GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc/PANI, and GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI are 27, 124, 179, 195, and 295 mAh g−1, respectively. The drastic increase in the specific capacity of the ternary nanocomposite compared to the pure components and the binary composite is due to the synergistic effect between the individual components. In addition, the combination of GQDs-PolyFc and PANI provides a conductive layer and bonding to Fe3O4, which could efficiently increase the electron and ion transfer at the interface between electrode and electrolyte, leading to the improvement of specific capacity and rate performance. Fast charging is another important element of an energy storage device that is urgently needed in practical applications. So, the final ternary nanocomposite is then subjected to GCD at various current densities (2.5–10 A g−1) and the obtained curves are shown in Fig. 9b. The shape of the GCD plot is maintained with increasing current density, indicating the feasibility of fast charging with a faradaic battery-type redox reaction. Additionally, regardless of the applied current density, the charge and discharge times are equal, indicating that the CE is maintained at different charging rates. Also, the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode has an imperceptible IR drop at all current densities. The specific capacity of all electrode materials at different current densities was estimated and is shown in Fig. 9c and Table 1 . It can be clearly seen that the value of specific capacity decreases at higher current densities because the electrolyte ions do not have enough time to penetrate into the active sites of the electrodes. The specific capacity of the final ternary nanocomposite presents considerable superiority over many previously reported GQDs-based electrodes, which are listed in Table 2 . Moreover, the charge storage retention of the final ternary nanocomposite (GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode) with cycling was then investigated (Fig. 9d). After 5000 GCD cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode can retain 91.45% of its initial capacity. This can be attributed to the properties of the final nanocomposite, which has excellent micro- and macrostructure, better wettability and higher electrode conductivity. Fig. 9d shows the coulombic efficiency (CE) of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI electrode, estimated from Eq. (3), and reveals that it remained stable, at almost 99% after 5000 cycles, indicating superior reversibility of the redox reactions taking place in the nanocomposite and not involving parasitic reactions.

Conductivity and ion transfer kinetics are well evaluated using EIS experiments performed in a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz. The Nyquist curves for the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4, GQDs-PolyFc/PANI, and GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI samples are shown in Fig. 10 . The Nyquist curve consists essentially of three parts. At high frequencies, the x-intercept on the real axis represents the effective series resistance (ESR), which indicates the solution resistance, contact resistance, and inherent resistance of the electrode material. As can be seen from Fig. 10, all electrodes have very low ESR. The second section, the semicircle in the mid-frequency range, illustrates the charge transport resistance (Rct) at the surface of the active materials and electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 10, the Rct values of the samples decrease in the order of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4 > GQDs-PolyFc/PANI > GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI, indicating an increased conductivity of the final ternary nanocomposite. The EIS results are consistent with the results from CV and GCD described above. The third section is the vertical line in the low frequency region showing the ion diffusion resistance. From Fig. 10, it can be seen that the final ternary nanocomposite has a good capacitive property compared to other nanocomposites.

Fig. 11 a shows a schematic diagram of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI//GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI symmetrical cell. To determine the stable operating voltage window of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI//GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI symmetric cell, the CV curves were measured with different working potentials (see Fig. 11b). This symmetric cell has stable performance at a voltage window below 1.7 V and sudden polarization occurs above 1.7 V. Therefore, the capacitive behavior and electrochemical properties of the fabricated cell were evaluated using CV and GCD experiments at the obtained stable potential window. Fig. 11c shows the CV plots of the fabricated device at different scan rates. The CV plots have a quasi-rectangular shape at all scan rates of 10–100 mV s−1. The CV plots also show similar shapes, indicating excellent rate capability. GCD curves of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI//GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI symmetric cell at various current densities of 2.5–10 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 11d. The fabricated cell exhibits symmetric and triangular GCD profile with a tiny IR drop. The estimated specific capacity of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI//GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI symmetric cell shows a high capacity of 73.1 mAh g−1 at 2.5 A g−1, indicating that our fabricated symmetric cell is able to supply outstanding energy storage device. Fig. 11e shows the cycling performance of the fabricated device after 2000 GCD cycles. The charge storage retains about 91.87% of the initial capacity, implying excellent cycling stability. Power density and energy density are two important factors for all energy storage devices, indicating the potential of the device. The energy density (Wh kg−1) and power density (W kg−1) of the symmetric cell were calculated using the Eqs. (4) and (5). The calculated energy and power densities of GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI//GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI symmetric cell are presented as a Ragone diagram, as shown in Fig. 11f. The GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI//GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI symmetric cell shows a high energy density 62.1 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 4140 W kg−1. These values are higher than those symmetrical cells reported previously, such as CA-PANI (25 Wh kg−1 at 1018 W kg−1) [85], C-Fe/PANI (41.3 Wh kg−1 at 231.9 W kg−1) [86], PANI-AB (15.7 Wh kg−1 at 600 W kg−1) [87], and PANI-1-FeCN (24.34 Wh kg−1 at 121.69 W kg−1) [88], indicating excellent electrochemical performance of the fabricated device.

GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI ternary nanocomposite has been prepared by a simple physical mixing method. The preparation of all samples has been approved using various techniques such as NMR, FT-IR, FE-SEM, EDX and XRD. As-synthesized ternary nanocomposite shows outstanding supercapacitor performance over the individual components. Modification of the surface of the GQDs with Fc-containing polymer improved its electrochemical performance. Also, the greatly improved specific capacity of the ternary nanocomposite is due to the introduction of PANI and Fe3O4 into the GQDs-PolyFc. The GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI ternary nanocomposite has a specific capacity of 295 mAh g−1 at 2.5 A g−1 in a three-electrode system demonstrated. To confirm the practical application of the as-prepared nanocomposite, GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI//GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI symmetric cell was constructed. The assembled symmetric supercapacitor exhibits excellent energy and power density up to 62.1 Wh kg−1, and 4140 W kg−1, respectively. Moreover, acceptable charge storage retention of 91.45% after 5000 cycles was achieved. The significant electrochemical performance of the GQDs-PolyFc/Fe3O4/PANI ternary nanocomposite ensures its future application in energy storage devices.

Perspectives of the further development of ferrocenyl-based nanomaterials for the energy storage devices purposes can be related to two important directions. The first one is concerned with the improvement of inherent behaviors of nanomaterials, including utilization of novel conjugated derivatives of Fc, copolymers with other redox-active materials, incorporation of additional components to the nanocomposite, and development of new nanostructures. The second direction is related to a variety of pathways utilizing complete electrodes in supercapacitors. Fabricated asymmetric supercapacitors, which reveals remarkable electrochemical performances compared to the symmetric device, or construct battery-supercapacitor hybrid devices that can be leads to considerable energy and power densities.
Elmira Payami: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Software. Reza Teimuri-Mofrad: Supervision, Project administration, Data curation.
